transcript:
Here's what you're watching: a bright blue cluster at the center slowly hollows itself out, sending pale motes drifting along curved paths toward a ring of dim satellite clusters — which brighten as the center dims, like energy changing hands.

But look at the group names in the code, because they're doing real philosophical work. The center's layer is called "what departs," the traveling motes sit in a group called "what travels," and where they land is "what arrives" — three different words for what could just be "the same particle," because the piece won't commit to that continuity. And at each destination, there's a structure the artist calls dormant rings — hollow circles that were sitting there from frame one, before anything arrived. When a traveler lands, the code doesn't just draw a new dot: it brightens those pre-existing rings, described in a comment as "did arrival transmit, or merely activate?"

So the destinations were never empty. They were already full of latent shapes, just waiting to be lit. The piece isn't showing you a signal being sent — it's showing you that it genuinely can't tell you whether anything was sent at all.
